museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: January reveals the next Nintendo machine while the release shelf warms up.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for January 2025. This is recent history, so dates and interpretation should be rechecked during human edit.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where possible.

items.title: Dynasty Warriors: Origins
items.body: Omega Force reintroduces the crowd-battle spectacle at the start of a packed year.
items.sourceNote: Shacknews lists January 17, 2025.

items.title: Final Fantasy VII Rebirth
items.body: Square's second remake chapter reaches PC players.

items.title: Sniper Elite: Resistance
items.body: Rebellion gives January a familiar British stealth-action presence.

items.title: Citizen Sleeper 2: Starward Vector
items.body: A narrative RPG about debt, bodies and survival makes the month feel more intimate.

items.title: Kingdom Come: Deliverance II
items.body: Warhorse returns to medieval mud, systems and argument.

items.title: Civilization VII
items.body: Firaxis reopens the one-more-turn museum case.

items.title: Avowed
items.body: Obsidian brings a first-person RPG to Game Pass and the February pile-up.

items.title: Monster Hunter Wilds
items.body: Capcom makes hunting feel global, cross-platform and unignorable.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, subscription and buying context around the month.

items.title: Nintendo Switch 2 becomes the year's hardware spine
items.body: From January reveal to April Direct to June launch, the year is organised around Nintendo's successor machine.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo sources confirm January reveal, April Direct and June 5 launch.

items.title: UK pricing turns hardware into household arithmetic
items.body: Switch 2's UK price and launch software make the console feel like a considered family purchase rather than impulse tech.
items.sourceNote: UK-facing Nintendo Direct and retailer coverage.

items.title: Game Pass remains a release-window lens
items.body: Avowed, Doom, Clair Obscur and other releases keep subscription access central to how players talk about value.
items.sourceNote: Broad 2025 platform context.

items.title: PC handhelds and portable play keep pressure on consoles
items.body: The Steam Deck/Switch 2 comparison makes handheld performance a routine part of launch conversation.
items.sourceNote: Hardware context; not tied to a single date.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players discovered, argued about and shared the month.

items.title: The algorithm is the newsagent
items.body: Trailers, Directs, showcases and short-form clips now do the work that magazine covers once did.

items.title: Discord is the playground
items.body: Release-day talk gathers in servers, group chats, voice calls and spoiler-muted channels.

items.title: Wishlist culture becomes anticipation
items.body: Steam wishlists, Game Pass queues and eShop preloads turn wanting a game into a visible ritual.

items.title: Launches are patched events
items.body: A game rarely arrives as a fixed object; day-one updates, roadmaps and hotfixes sit beside the release date.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading from the player side of recent history.

items.title: The year felt like a handover
items.body: Switch 2 made the old Switch feel historical almost overnight, even while its library still mattered.

items.title: Everything was both new and remembered
items.body: Oblivion, Metal Gear, Tony Hawk, Shinobi and Metroid sat beside Clair Obscur and Silksong.

items.title: UK players watched value closely
items.body: Hardware price, subscriptions, physical editions and digital libraries all became part of the emotional texture.

items.title: The release calendar felt unusually dense
items.body: By autumn, choosing what not to play became part of being a player.
